title: Advanced sl3 Integration
text: Use make_hte3_Task_tx() want supply nuisance learners work directly lower-level learner objects.
code: library(hte3) library(sl3)  data <- hte3_example_data(n = 100, seed = 4)  task <- make_hte3_Task_tx(   data = data,   modifiers = c("W1", "W2"),   confounders = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),   treatment = "A",   outcome = "Y",   learner_pi = Lrnr_mean$new(),   learner_mu = Lrnr_mean$new(),   learner_m = Lrnr_mean$new(),   cross_fit_and_cv = FALSE )

title: Advanced sl3 Integration
text: right level want exact learner object rather wrapper returned fit_cate() fit_crr().
code: learner <- Lrnr_cate_DR$new(base_learner = Lrnr_mean$new()) trained <- learner$train(task)  head(predict_hte3(trained, data)) #> [1] 1.553732 1.553732 1.553732 1.553732 1.553732 1.553732

text: can cross-validate low-level learner portfolios directly. analogous CRR workflow :

title: Advanced sl3 Integration
text: cross_fit_and_cv make_hte3_Task_tx() controls nuisance estimation task stage. modifiers = V confounders = W V strict subset W, reduced-modifier target E[Y(1) - Y(0) | V] = E[tau(W) | V]. reduced-modifier setting, current low-level R-learner targets overlap-weighted projection rather unweighted V-conditional CATE. continuous-treatment CATE, low-level Lrnr_cate_R path one currently implemented. EP-specific sieve construction tuning, see EP-Learner Sieve Tuning. building new learner, see Extending hte3 Custom Learners.

title: CATE Workflow
text: fit_cate() supports method = c("dr", "r", "t", "ep"). method = "dr" main difference-scale default treatment binary categorical want target-aligned CATE fit uses propensity outcome nuisance estimates. method = "r" residual--residual path current continuous-treatment option. reduced-modifier settings, targets overlap-weighted projection rather unweighted E[Y(1) - Y(0) | V]. method = "t" baseline fits outcome models treatment arm subtracts . method = "ep" sieve-based plug-path. Use want EP family; see EP-Learner Sieve Tuning basis-size tuning EP-DR versus EP-R details.

title: CATE Workflow
text: modifiers = V confounders = W V strict subset W, target interest E[Y(1) - Y(0) | V] = E[tau(W) | V]. supported binary/categorical-treatment setting: DR, EP, default two-stage T-learner align target current R-learner instead targets overlap-weighted projection onto functions V fit_cate(..., method = "r") warns reduced-modifier settings.

title: CATE Workflow
text: Wrapper-level CATE cross-validation controlled cross_validate cv_control. summary(cv_model) reports selected learner. selected learner EP candidate, summary also reports selected basis size EP selection metadata.
code: cv_model <- fit_cate(   task,   method = c("dr", "r", "ep"),   base_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   cross_validate = TRUE,   cv_control = list(V = 2) )  cv_model #> <hte3_model> #>   Target: CATE #>   Engine: sl3 #>   Method: portfolio(dr, r, ep) #>   Cross-validated: yes #>   Selected candidate: r #>   Selected method: r #>   EP basis grid: 2, 4, 8, 12, 16 #>   Modifiers: W1, W2 head(predict(cv_model, data)) #> [1] 1.305974 1.305974 1.305974 1.305974 1.305974 1.305974

title: CATE Workflow
text: Continuous-treatment CATE currently supports method = "r" , using partially linear effect model * tau(X). Use sieve_num_basis want one fixed EP basis size sieve_basis_grid want wrapper-level EP basis-size selection. direct control candidate learner objects, see Advanced sl3 Integration.

title: CRR Workflow
text: fit_crr() supports method = c("ep", "ipw", "t"). method = "ep" default flexible CRR path. method = "ipw" weighting-based benchmark places emphasis propensity model. method = "t" baseline built arm-specific outcome models risk-ratio scale. wrapper pattern CATE: build hte3_Task, choose CRR learner family method, optionally compare candidates cross_validate = TRUE.

title: CRR Workflow
text: method = "ep" cross_validate = TRUE, wrapper also compares EP fits across basis sizes. Use sieve_basis_grid want control grid explicitly, use sieve_num_basis one fixed EP fit.
code: cv_model <- fit_crr(   task,   method = c("ipw", "t", "ep"),   base_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   cross_validate = TRUE,   cv_control = list(V = 2) )  cv_model #> <hte3_model> #>   Target: CRR #>   Engine: sl3 #>   Method: portfolio(ipw, t, ep) #>   Cross-validated: yes #>   Selected candidate: ep[basis=24, interaction=3] #>   Selected method: ep #>   Selected EP basis size: 24 #>   EP basis grid: 3, 6, 12, 18, 24 #>   Modifiers: W1, W2, W3 head(predict(cv_model, data)) #> [1] 0.151008 0.151008 0.151008 0.151008 0.151008 0.151008

title: CRR Workflow
text: CRR workflows require non-negative outcome. cross_fit hte_task() controls nuisance estimation separate wrapper-level learner selection. low-level CRR learner portfolios, see Advanced sl3 Integration.

title: EP-Learner Sieve Tuning
text: CATE, hte3 now exposes two EP variants Lrnr_cate_EP(..., targeting_style = ...): targeting_style = "dr" original EP update acts plug-analogue DR-learner. targeting_style = "r" uses overlap-weighted targeting objective acts plug-analogue R-learner. cases, Sieve package used build basis matrix. EP update fit glmnet inside Lrnr_cate_EP Lrnr_crr_EP. evaluating basis, Sieve rescales modifier [0, 1]. current hte3 implementation, sieve family fixed cosine basis functions, univariate building blocks 1, cos(pi x), cos(2 pi x), .... Multivariate basis functions tensor products univariate terms across modifiers. package currently expose alternative Sieve basis families wrapper learner level. practical tuning knobs : sieve_num_basis: sieve size one fixed EP fit sieve_basis_grid: basis-size grid wrapper-level EP cross-validation sieve_interaction_order: maximum interaction order screen_basis_with_lasso: experimental CATE-alternative explicit sieve-grid cross-validation targeting_style: choose EP-DR EP-R CATE r_targeting_basis: choose EP-R first-stage basis, either full W reduced V + e(W) construction sieve_num_basis = NULL, hte3 uses default ceiling(n^(1/3) * d), n sample size d dimension first-stage EP sieve covariates.

title: EP-Learner Sieve Tuning
text: important difference first-stage targeting objective: EP-DR uses original inverse-propensity-weighted targeting step sieve built modifier set V. EP-R starts plug-effect tau0.hat(W) = mu.hat(1, W) - mu.hat(0, W) fits overlap-weighted residual update. EP-R, can choose two first-stage basis constructions: r_targeting_basis = "v_plus_propensity": default; build first-stage sieve modifier set V plus treated-arm propensity score e(W) r_targeting_basis = "full_w": build first-stage sieve full confounder set W v_plus_propensity option dimension-reduced EP-R path: uses scalar treated-arm propensity score 1D summary W still allowing first-stage sieve interact score modifier set. mode, effective first-stage interaction order always least 2. practical target distinction : EP-DR keeps usual V-conditional CATE target supported binary/categorical setting. EP-R can stable severe overlap violations, reduced-modifier settings generally targets overlap-weighted projection rather unweighted E[Y(1) - Y(0) | V]. EP-R currently supports binary-treatment CATE tasks . Lrnr_crr_EP remains unchanged.

title: EP-Learner Sieve Tuning
text: high-level wrapper, EP tuning now follows two simple rules: Use sieve_num_basis cross_validate = FALSE want one fixed sieve size. Use sieve_basis_grid cross_validate = TRUE want wrapper compare multiple EP candidates. CATE EP learners, wrapper-level tuning still uses DR selector loss cross_validate_cate(), even targeting_style = "r". example: can also ask wrapper compare EP-DR EP-R directly: sieve_basis_grid = NULL, wrapper uses heuristic default grid c(d, 2d, 4d, 6d, 8d), d first-stage EP sieve dimension chosen variant. fitted model summary reports selected candidate, selected EP targeting style, selected EP basis size, EP grid compared.
code: ep_cv <- fit_cate(   hte_task(     data = data,     modifiers = c("W1", "W2"),     confounders = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),     treatment = "A",     outcome = "Y",     propensity_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),     outcome_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),     mean_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),     cross_fit = FALSE   ),   method = "ep",   base_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   cross_validate = TRUE,   cv_control = list(V = 2),   ep_targeting_style = "dr",   sieve_basis_grid = c(2, 4, 6, 8) )  summary(ep_cv) #> <summary.hte3_model> #>   Target: CATE #>   Engine: sl3 #>   Method: ep #>   Cross-validated: yes #>   Rows: 120 #>   Selected candidate: ep[style=dr, basis=8, interaction=3] #>   Selected method: ep #>   Selected EP basis size: 8 #>   EP targeting style: dr #>   EP basis grid: 2, 4, 6, 8 #>   Modifiers: W1, W2 #>   Treatment variable: A ep_style_cv <- suppressWarnings(fit_cate(   hte_task(     data = data,     modifiers = c("W1", "W2"),     confounders = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),     treatment = "A",     outcome = "Y",     propensity_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),     outcome_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),     mean_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),     cross_fit = FALSE   ),   method = "ep",   base_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   cross_validate = TRUE,   cv_control = list(V = 2),   sieve_basis_grid = c(2, 4, 6, 8),   ep_targeting_style = c("dr", "r") )) #> Error : x has missing values; title: EP-Learner Sieve Tuning
text: Use grid control flexibility-compute tradeoff: Smaller grids faster usually safer moderate sample sizes. Larger grids give EP correction flexibility can become noisier higher-dimensional modifier spaces. Lower sieve_interaction_order values reduce complexity modifier dimension large. practice: start default heuristic grid want simple first pass use sieve_basis_grid = (3:8) * d match low-dimensional paper grid use smaller additive grids (1:6) * d sieve_interaction_order = 1 modifier dimension high

title: EP-Learner Sieve Tuning
text: Sieve orders multivariate basis increasing index-product complexity. practice, means constant term comes first, lower-frequency univariate terms, higher-frequency interaction terms. argument sieve_interaction_order limits many coordinates can vary inside single tensor-product basis term: sieve_interaction_order = 1 gives additive sieve sieve_interaction_order = 2 allows pairwise interactions sieve_interaction_order = 3 allows three-way interactions two modifiers, early cosine basis terms look like constant, terms like cos(pi x1) cos(pi x2), higher-frequency terms, interaction terms cos(pi x1) * cos(pi x2).

title: EP-Learner Sieve Tuning
text: direct low-level EP fit: corresponding EP-R fit dimension-reduced first-stage basis: practice, paper often paired EP-learner richer base-learner stacks. examples keep Lrnr_mean$new() sieve tuning pattern easy read; can replace base_learner sl3 learner stack.
code: ep_fit <- Lrnr_cate_EP$new(   base_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   sieve_num_basis = 4,   sieve_interaction_order = 2 )$train(task) #> Warning in learner$predict_fold(learner_task, fold_number): #> Lrnr_independent_binomial is not cv-aware: self$predict_fold reverts to #> self$predict #> Warning in learner$predict_fold(learner_task, fold_number): strat_A_Lrnr_mean #> is not cv-aware: self$predict_fold reverts to self$predict #> Warning in learner$predict_fold(learner_task, fold_number): Lrnr_mean is not #> cv-aware: self$predict_fold reverts to self$predict  head(predict_hte3(ep_fit, data)) #> [1] 1.429171 1.429171 1.429171 1.429171 1.429171 1.429171 ep_r_fit <- Lrnr_cate_EP$new(   base_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   sieve_num_basis = 4,   sieve_interaction_order = 1,   targeting_style = "r",   r_targeting_basis = "v_plus_propensity" )$train(task)  head(predict_hte3(ep_r_fit, data))

title: Causal Forests with GRF
text: Use grf_cate() target conditional mean difference. Use grf_crr() target conditional risk ratio outcome non-negative.

title: Causal Forests with GRF
text: grf_cate() supports method = c("dr", "r", "ep"). method = "r" direct causal-forest path trains grf::causal_forest(). method = "dr" uses DR-learner GRF nuisance base learners. method = "ep" uses EP-learner GRF nuisance base learners. Within EP family, ep_targeting_style = "dr" selects EP-DR ep_targeting_style = "r" selects EP-R. pass ep_targeting_style = c("dr", "r"), wrapper-level cross-validation can compare EP variants. omit method cross_validate, grf_cate() compares default EP-R candidate direct GRF R-learner.
code: data <- hte3_example_data(n = 200, seed = 1)  fit <- grf_cate(   data = data,   modifiers = c("W1", "W2"),   confounders = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),   treatment = "A",   outcome = "Y",   method = "r",   cross_fit = FALSE )  head(predict(fit, data)) summary(fit)

title: Causal Forests with GRF
text: grf_crr() supports method = c("ep", "ipw", "t"). omit method, wrapper fits one EP model.
code: fit <- grf_crr(   data = data,   modifiers = c("W1", "W2"),   confounders = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),   treatment = "A",   outcome = "Y_binary",   method = "ep",   cross_fit = FALSE )  head(predict(fit, data)) summary(fit)

title: Causal Forests with GRF
text: GRF wrappers accept direct nuisance estimates: mu.hat: n x 2 matrix ordered (control, treatment) pi.hat: length-n vector P(= treatment_level | X) m.hat: optional length-n vector marginal outcome means m.hat omitted mu.hat pi.hat supplied, hte3 computes m.hat internally.
code: mu.hat <- cbind(data$mu0, data$mu1) pi.hat <- data$pi1  fit <- grf_cate(   data = data,   modifiers = c("W1", "W2"),   confounders = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),   treatment = "A",   outcome = "Y",   mu.hat = mu.hat,   pi.hat = pi.hat,   cross_fit = FALSE )

title: Causal Forests with GRF
text: GRF-specific arguments worth understanding : cross_fit folds nuisance estimation cross_validate cv_control outer learner selection ep_targeting_style ep_r_targeting_basis GRF-backed EP variants treatment_level control_level treatment labels default 1 0 grf_params GRF hyperparameters num.trees min.node.size tune amount GRF tuning: "light", "none", "" GRF learners, tune = "light" tunes sample.fraction, mtry, min.node.size modest search budget. tune = "none" disables tuning, tune = "" requests GRF’s full tuning path.

text: params list parameters meta-learning algorithm. base_learner sl3 learner object inheriting Lrnr_base specifies base supervised learning algorithm used meta-learner. transform_function function transform predictions base learner. Default identity transform. pseudo_outcome_type outcome type pseudo-outcome used meta-learner. Options c("continuous", "binomial", "quasibinomial"). text: modifiers = V confounders = W, natural CATE target E[Y(1) - Y(0) | V] = E[tau(W) | V]. supported binary/categorical-treatment setting, DR-, EP-, default two-stage T-learner align modifier-conditional target. current R-learner generally target object V strict subset W; warns fit time.

title: Create an hte3 Task with a Production-Oriented Interface — hte_task
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(sl3)  data <- hte3_example_data(n = 80, seed = 1)  task <- hte_task(   data = data,   modifiers = c("W1", "W2"),   confounders = c("W1", "W2", "W3"),   treatment = "A",   outcome = "Y",   propensity_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   outcome_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   mean_learner = Lrnr_mean$new(),   cross_fit = FALSE )  task } # }

title: Truncate and Calibrate Propensity Scores — truncate_propensity
text: truncation_method parameter controls propensity scores calibrated truncated. - "isotonic": Performs isotonic calibration, providing data-adaptive truncation calibration propensity scores. - "adaptive": Adapts truncation level using loss function inverse propensity score. - "deterministic": Bounds estimates away 0 1 using threshold 25/sqrt(n)/log(n). - "none": Bounds estimates away 0 1 using threshold 1/n.

title: hte3 0.1.1
text: Hardened default get_autoML() stack high-level API uses available safe subset learners warns optional runtime packages missing. Finished two-stage T-learner update reduced-modifier settings first-stage regressions use confounder set W default second stage projects onto modifier set V. Tightened task validation folds, weights, treatment values, user-supplied nuisance estimates. Added release checks custom website, vignette inventory, package/docs sync, excluding deployed site assets package builds.
